data.title: Open The Door To Owens Corning's Upside Potential
data.description:
Navigating the market noise requires a disciplined approach. By digging into the underlying financials, investors can uncover the true value of a stock – an essential skill for those looking to weather volatility, particularly with dividend-paying investments.

A particularly risky class of dividend stocks is those that offer decent yields and are backed by fundamentally sound businesses, yet trade at valuations that far exceed their true economic worth. These “dividend-trap” stocks carry extra risk with limited upside potential.

Our latest Long Idea is the opposite of a trap. This stock is positioned to benefit from the aging housing market and growth in its core markets. The company has strong fundamentals, returns ample capital to shareholders, and best of all, its stock is cheap.

We originally made Owens Corning (NYSE:OC) a Long Idea in January 2022 and most recently reiterated our thesis in July 2024. The stock has outperformed the S&P 500 since our original report. Even so, our thesis on this stock remains intact and additional upside potential remains.

OC offers favorable Risk/Reward based on the company’s:

  • position to benefit from an aging housing market,
  • growing core markets,
  • strong fundamentals and shareholder return,
  • quality executive compensation, and
  • cheap stock valuation.

What’s Working

Benefit From the Aging Housing Market

One of the most significant industry tailwinds driving Owens Corning is the aging housing stock in the U.S. The typical home hit a record age of 36 years old in 2024, which is up from 27 years in 2012. This increase in age of homes is due to the lack of new supply (fewer homes being built) and the better affordability of older homes.

The U.S. has been building fewer homes since the construction industry took a hit in 2008. Only 9% of U.S. homes were built in the 2010’s, which represents the lowest share of any one decade since the 1940’s.

While Owens Corning can sell products for new construction, the rising age of homes creates even more demand for Owens Corning’s remodel and repair products, such as roofing, insulation, and doors.

Figure 1: Median Age of U.S. Homes: 2012 – 2024

Source: Redfin

Growing Markets Provides Long-Term Opportunities

On February 14, 2025, Owens Corning signed a definitive agreement to sell its glass reinforcement business (previously reported in the Composites segment) to Praana Group. The sale completes the company’s strategic review of the business and focuses resources into the residential and commercial building products markets in North America and Europe.

As part of this strategic review, Owens Corning now has three reportable business segments: Roofing, Insulation, and Doors. Best of all, each of these three markets are forecasted to grow through the next decade:

  • The global roofing market is forecast to grow 4.3% compounded annually through 2034.
  • The global insulation market is forecast to grow 7.7% compounded annually through 2034.
  • The global doors market is forecast to grow 4.6% compounded annually through 2034.

As an existing industry leader, Owens Corning is in a great position to take advantage of the growth in each of the segments. Importantly, Owens Corning is not dependent on any one of these markets, as its business is well diversified across the three.

1Q25, Owens Corning generated 44% of its revenue from its roofing business, 35% from its insulation business, and 21% from its doors business. See Figure 2.

Figure 2: Owens Corning’s Revenue by Segment in 1Q25

Strong Fundamentals Across Decades

Owens Corning has grown revenue and net operating profit after-tax (NOPAT) by 8% and 17% compounded annually since 2014, respectively. See Figure 3.

The company improved its NOPAT margin from 6% in 2014 to 14% in the TTM while invested capital turns remained the same at 0.8 over the same time. Rising NOPAT margins drive return on invested capital (ROIC) from 4% in 2014 to 11% in the TTM.

Additionally, the company’s Core Earnings grew 20% compounded annually from $209 million in 2014 to $1.4 billion in the TTM.

Figure 3: Owens Corning’s Revenue and NOPAT Since 2014

Executives Are Aligned with Shareholders

Corporate governance plays a crucial role in finding good stocks because it shows us how well executives’ interests are aligned with shareholders’ interests. By evaluating executive compensation plans, we can identify companies with quality corporate governance, such as Owens Corning.

Owens Corning’s executive compensation plan aligns the interests of executives and shareholders by tying one third of its long-term performance share awards to return on capital (ROC), a variation of ROIC.

As we mentioned in past reports, there is a strong correlation between improving ROIC and increasing shareholder value. Even though the company’s executive compensation plans could be improved by increasing the weighting of ROC or using ROIC as the main performance goal, the company’s inclusion of ROC, as a performance goal has helped create shareholder value by driving higher ROIC and economic earnings.

Per Figure 4, Owens Corning’s ROIC has increased from 4% in 2014 to 11% in the TTM. Economic earnings rose from -$104 million to $409 million over the same time.

Figure 4: Owens Corning’s ROIC and Economic Earnings Since 2014

Attractive Dividend and Repurchase Yield of 5%+

Since 2019, Owens Corning has paid $898 million (8% of market cap) in cumulative dividends and has increased its quarterly dividend from $0.22/share in 1Q19 to $0.69/share in 1Q25. The company’s current dividend, when annualized, provides a 1.9% yield.

Owens Corning also returns capital to shareholders through share repurchases. From 2019 through 1Q25, the company repurchased $2.8 billion (24% of market cap) worth of shares.

In May 2025, Owens Corning’s board of directors authorized a new 12 million share repurchase program. With the 5.7 million shares remaining from the previous authorization, the company currently is authorized to repurchase a total of 17.7 million shares.

Should the company repurchase shares at its TTM rate, it would repurchase $414 million of shares over the next year, which equals 3.5% of the current market cap. When combined, the dividend and share repurchase yield could reach 5.4%.

Strong Cash Flow Generation

We believe Owens Corning will be able to afford to pay its dividends and repurchase shares because of its large free cash flow (FCF). From 2019 through 2023, Owens corning generated $4.6 billion in FCF, which equals 25% of the company’s enterprise value.

The company completed its acquisition of Masonite in 2Q24, which led to FCF of -$3.7 billion in the quarter.

In each quarter since the acquisition (3Q24, 4Q24, and 1Q25), Owens Corning’s free cash flow has been positive and rising quarter-over-quarter (QoQ). Given the company’s consistent margins and steadily growing markets, we would expect free cash flow generation to continue rising in the future.

Overall, even with the acquisition, Owens Corning generated a cumulative $2.6 billion in FCF from 2019-1Q25. See Figure 5.

Figure 5: Owens Corning’s Cumulative FCF: 2019 – TTM

Owens Corning’s past repurchases have also meaningfully reduced its shares outstanding from 109 million in 2019 to 85 million at the end of 1Q25. See Figure 6.

Figure 6: Owens Corning’s Shares Outstanding: 2019 – 1Q25

Great Margins

Owens Corning is vertically integrated across each of its business segments. This integration creates efficiencies that manifest in consistently high profit margins.

Per Figure 7, Owens Corning has the second highest NOPAT margin among its competitors, which include Carlisle Companies (CSL), Cabot Corp (CBT), Dupont De Nemours (DD), and Hunstman Corp (HUN).

Figure 7: Owens Corning’ Profitability Vs. Peers: TTM

What’s Not Working

Slowdown in Home Sales

The current economic uncertainty is hurting consumer spending, especially when it comes to home purchases. According to Redfin, homes are selling at the slowest pace in the last six years, as demand is waning and homes remain expensive in the eyes of potential homebuyers. Additionally, $698 billion worth of homes are currently for sale, which represents an all-time high and an increase of 20% from a year ago.

A freezing of the home sale market could lead to (1) consumers slowing remodel plans, as sellers don’t want to spend on a home they plan to sell or (2) cautious owners who want to delay a remodel until the economy improves.

Management alluded to the cautious mindset of consumers in the company’s 1Q25 earnings call when they noted residential remodel activity is expected to remain weaker through the first half of the year.

However, while activity might be weaker in the short-term, the good news is the leading indicator of remodeling activity (LIRA) projects that remodeling activity in the U.S. will increase by 2.5% year-over-year in the first quarter of 2026.

Additionally, even if remodeling activity remains sluggish longer than forecasted, any potential downside to Owens Corning’s business is already priced into its current stock price, as we’ll show below.

Current Price Implies Permanent Profit Decline

At its current price of $140/share, OC has a price-to-economic book value (PEBV) ratio of 0.9. This ratio means the market expects the company’s profits to permanently decline 10% from TTM levels. For context, Owens Corning has grown NOPAT by 19% compounded annually over the last five years and 17% compounded annually over the last decade.

Below, we use our reverse discounted cash flow (DCF) model to analyze expectations for different stock price scenarios for OC.

In the first scenario, we quantify the expectations baked into the current price. If we assume:

  • NOPAT margin immediately falls to 11% (below five-year average of 13% and TTM NOPAT margin of 14%) through 2034, and
  • revenue grows 4% a year through 2034 (compared to 9% CAGR over the last five years and 8% CAGR over the last decade) then

the stock would be worth $140/share today – nearly equal to the current stock price. In this scenario, revenue grows at the low end of the company’s end-market forecast growth. Additionally, Owens Corning’s NOPAT would grow just 1% compounded annually from 2025 – 2034, which is far below the company’s historical NOPAT growth rates.

Shares Could Go 20%+ Higher Even If Profits Grow Far Slower Than Historical Rates

If we instead assume:

  • NOPAT margin falls to 12% (still below five-year average of 13% and TTM NOPAT margin of 14%) through 2034,
  • revenue grows 4% a year through 2034 (compared to 9% CAGR over the last five years and 8% CAGR over the last decade) then

the stock would be worth $171/share today – a ~20% upside to the current price. In this scenario, Owens Corning’s NOPAT would grow just 2% compounded annually through fiscal 2034, which would still be well below the company’s historical growth rates dating back to 2007. Should NOPAT grow closer to historical rates, the upside is even larger.

Figure 8 compares Owens Corning’s historical NOPAT to the NOPAT implied in each of the above scenarios.

Figure 8: Owens Corning’s Historical and Implied NOPAT: DCF Valuation Scenarios

This article was originally published on June 11, 2025.

data.title: Argentine Banks: Volatility Is Not A Symptom Of Weakness, But Part Of The Ecosystem
data.description:
Investment Thesis: pause, not retreat

For months, Argentine banks listed on the NYSE were protagonists of one of the most intense rallies in the emerging universe.

Since the arrival of Javier Milei to power and the expectation of a sustained macroeconomic normalization, stocks such as Banco Macro (BMA), BBVA Argentina (BBAR), Grupo Financiero Galicia (GGAL) and Grupo Supervielle (SUPV), which I have previously analyzed, multiplied their value in dollars, recovering levels that seemed impossible just a year ago.

But in July, the tide turned. Without warning, and without companies reporting substantial changes in their results, the market began to punish the sector with force. For those who do not follow the Argentine case closely, the fall may seem a symptom of deterioration. For those who know the cyclical history of their economy, this correction is almost a compulsory chapter within the same book: that of structural volatility.

In this scenario, I believe the most prudent option is to hold assets while waiting for new signals.

What happened? A combination of noisy signals that amplified perceived risk

The fall in Argentine bank stocks in July did not respond to a deterioration in the sector's operating fundamentals. There were no signs of an increase in late payment, no significant contraction in financial margins, and not even a substantial drop in quarterly results. Indeed, banks maintain healthy levels of capitalization, efficiency and liquidity. The abrupt change in market mood responded, instead, to a sequence of exogenous events that generated noise and uncertainty.

The first of these events was the alleged government intervention in the FX market. After months of an orthodox liberal discourse, in which the government had dismantled traps, cut monetary issuance and accumulated reserves, reports began to circulate that the Central Bank would have intervened to contain the financial dollar's price through covert purchases. This measure, interpreted by many as a contradiction of the announced course, triggered alarms among local and international investors.

Was it a technical correction to moderate weight appreciation? Or a symptom that internal tensions were forcing a shift in economic policy?

This was supplemented by technical market factors. After an exceptional rally in banking ADRs-with surges of over 300% in 12 months-, many institutional investors decided to take profits at the first sign of ambiguity. In a low-liquidity market such as the Argentine one, this rebalancing can be rapidly amplified. The volume traded in July showed simultaneous outflows from several funds, accelerating the correction without a clear signal from corporate balance sheets.

Finally, the impact of the political context cannot be ignored. The hardening of the conflict between the executive and some actors in Congress, the judicialization of key reforms and the increase in social unrest in certain sectors, fueled the perception that the cycle of reforms could not be sustained with the same strength. Institutional uncertainty, although not new in Argentina, is re-quoted every time political noise exceeds a certain threshold.

Taken together, these elements generated a dissonance between solid banking fundamentals and a country risk narrative that re-dominated asset prices. The correction, therefore, is not a reflection of structural weakness of the financial system, but of an external environment that -as so often in Argentina- acts as a catalyst for volatility.

An extraordinary rally followed by a logical correction

At the same time, the Argentine banks were coming off a meteoric rally. Since the lows of 2022, their dollar prices had multiplied by three, four or even more, in a context of reversal of expectations, risk compression and strong inflow of speculative flows after the political turn of December 2023. In 2025, the accumulated progress is still remarkable: BMA up +48.5%, GGAL +51%, BBAR +34.1% and SUPV +42.8%, despite having corrected between 5% and 7% during July.

This technical setback comes after a period of strong valuation improvements, with multiples still very low in relation to the ROE and the operating flow generated. SUPV, for example, is traded with a P/E FWD of just 4.4x and a P/B of 0.66x, while holding an ROE of 13.9% and a net margin above 12%. BMA, for its part, shows a projected ROE of 17% with a P/E of only 4.9x, well below the sector average in emerging markets, while maintaining capital ratios above 30% and an extremely efficient operating structure.

Even banks like BBAR, which corrected more strongly in the short term, show strong metrics: a P/E of 4.6x, an ROE of 15.5% and an ROE above 2.3%, along with a CFO that grew more than 60% year-on-year. In parallel, GGAL, which came to have one of the best momentum in the group, retains an ROE above 18%, a P/E FWD of 5.2x, and a controlled CAPEX that ensures efficiency amid uncertainty.

From a market point of view, the July correction is not surprising when looking at the momentum metrics in decline: BBAR and SUPV were rated "F" in performance at 3 and 6 months, with declines of between 30% and 45% in those periods. BMA, which also displays a note "F" at 3 and 6 months, maintains a positive performance in the year, although slower. This technical dynamic reflects a partial rotation of flows after the rally, rather than a change in fundamentals.

Valuation, growth and profitability: solid foundations in papers still punished

Beyond the technical adjustment observed in July, the financial fundamentals of Argentine banks continue to show signs of strength on three key fronts: attractive valuation multiples, sustained operational growth rate and high profitability against their share price. This triad is precisely the core of the thesis that continues to justify interest in the sector, even under scenarios of political noise or tactical correction.

From a valuation perspective, all banks analyzed are quoted with multiples significantly lower than their emerging market peers. With a P/E FWD of 4.9x and a P/B of 1.15x, BMA leads in efficiency without falling into overvaluation. GGAL, with a P/E of 5.2x and an ROE of 18%, offers one of the most interesting risk-return ratios in the sector.

SUPV continues to be the cheapest bank by multiples: quoted at just 3.9x earnings and 0.72x book value, while retaining a net margin of 12.3% and an ROE close to 14%. BBAR, with 4.6x P/E and 0.88x P/B, combines its attractiveness with an ROA of 2.3% and strong YoY growth in operating cash flow (+62.7%).

From the growth angle, all four banks show positive rates in EPS projections, led by BBAR (+50.8%) and BMA (+44.2%), reflecting not only the recovery of the credit business but also a substantial improvement in efficiency. SUPV and GGAL also project strong gains (+35.6% and +28.3% respectively), which, combined with low multiples, suggests room for price expansion.

In terms of profitability, ROE is the key metric for measuring efficiency: BMA and GGAL remain over 17% and SUPV and BBAR also exceed 13%. If you add the return on dividends (between 2.4% and 3.5% annualized), the total carry becomes relevant, especially in a positive real rate environment like the current one.

Macro environment: stabilization with no guarantee of continuity

At the macroeconomic level, the stabilization program achieved incredible progress: monthly inflation reduced to 1.5% in May, the lowest level in more than 5 years, and in June it was 1.6% (+39.4% YoY). Meanwhile, international reserves continue to be restocked.

However, these improvements were realized under exceptional conditions-contractive policy and capital controls still in place-leaving open the possibility of reversal if institutional confidence is not consolidated.

Political risk is once again at the front in the run-up to the legislative elections in October. Everything is in pause waiting for the results: the fragility of the parliamentary support, the social resistance to certain reforms and the possibility of institutional paralysis again put price on the uncertainty, even when the bank balances show solidity. A triumph of officialdom would allow it to advance into a second phase of deeper reforms, for which it needs more allies in Congress.

Conclusion: tactical pause, strategic opportunity

What is happening with the Argentine banks is not a collapse, but a pause. A noisy pause, yes. But I do not think it is a sign of structural weakness.

For the moment I consider that the best option is to hold, since in Argentina there is no prize without a shock. This has always been the case, and will continue to be so as long as political institutions do not accompany them with the same strength that these banks have silently and resiliently demonstrated.

data.title: WTI Crude Price Steadies Near $66 As Inventory Draw And Trade Hopes Ease Selling Pressure (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

West Texas Intermediate (WTI) crude oil futures are trading near $65.79 as of Thursday afternoon, recovering from a recent four-session decline. The modest rebound comes amid signs of easing trade tensions between the U.S. and European Union, alongside stronger-than-expected inventory data from the Energy Information Administration.

However, the broader price trend remains constrained below key resistance levels as traders await further clarity on global demand and macroeconomic developments. According to Wednesday’s EIA report, U.S. crude stockpiles fell by 3.2 million barrels, significantly more than the 2 million expected.

Gasoline inventories also declined by 1.7 million barrels, while distillates posted a 2.9 million barrel build. The net draw from crude and gasoline is seen as a bullish signal, reflecting solid end-user consumption, even as distillates suggest some downstream caution.

Price range holds as market eyes next catalyst

Technically, WTI crude remains within a well-defined range between $63.50 and $68.00, unable to decisively break either boundary. The price has found interim support near the lower bound of this zone, but resistance looms from the tightly packed 20/50/100/200 EMAs, which stretch from $66.07 to $68.12. Momentum is muted, with RSI (14) hovering at 46.81 and still below the neutral line.

Traders are also closely watching trade developments. U.S.–EU negotiations are reportedly advancing toward a deal that would reduce tariff threats on European imports. Meanwhile, upcoming discussions between U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and Chinese officials may influence global oil flows, especially if agreements emerge regarding China’s sourcing from sanctioned suppliers.

Outlook remains neutral until breakout above $68.50

For now, WTI crude remains trapped in a neutral-to-bearish technical structure, with only a break above $68.50 likely to trigger a broader trend reversal toward $72–$73. Downside risk persists if $63 fails to hold. Continued clarity on global trade, inventory trends, and Chinese demand will be key in determining the next leg for oil markets.

In our earlier outlooks, we highlighted the $63–$68 range as the key battleground for WTI crude prices. The current bounce off the lower edge follows the anticipated inventory-driven pivot, but broader direction will require resolution of trade-linked supply routes and a breach of EMA resistance.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

data.title: Logitech: Resilient And Strategically Positioned For Sustainable Growth
data.description:
Investment Summary

Logitech (NASDAQ:LOGI) is very well positioned, led by growth opportunities in its core markets (productivity peripherals, gaming, and video conferencing) and sound fundamentals with a balanced exposure in B2C (~60%) and B2B (~40%) markets. Their continuous effort towards product innovation & launches, and resilient consumer demand (despite pricing actions) reflects their brand reputation. On margin performance, despite global macroeconomic uncertainty and tariff headwinds, the company capitalizes on recent price action (reflecting strong consumer and enterprise brand equity), growth in core segments like gaming and video collaboration, and effective supply chain diversification. Logitech executes disciplined strategies, innovates new products, and proactively mitigates tariffs, suggesting that long-term growth remains intact. Although for the time being, the company has temporarily withdrawn FY26 guidance, I believe a return to formal FY guidance post-tariff stabilization could act as a near-term catalyst.

While some near-term headwinds are present here, a lot of that pessimism is already reflected by the stock’s current valuation. The growth story is considered compelling, and with the current valuation, the stock is currently trading at below its 5-year average forward P/E, which can be a potential entry point. Paired with solid fundamentals, brand value, cost measures, and execution, room for upside is definitely present.

Company Overview

Logitech is a more then 4-decade-old company with its roots from 1981 and is headquartered in Lausanne, Switzerland. LOGI designs and manufactures peripherals that allow people to connect, including keyboards, mice, webcams, video conferencing, gaming devices, keyboards and combos, pointing devices, webcams, headsets, and tablet accessories. The company earns 60% of its Revenue from B2C and 40% from B2B (enterprise) customers. In FY24, Logi derives ~44% revenue from the Americas, ~30% from EMEA, and ~25% from APAC. The company operates a highly diversified supply chain spanning six countries and continues to invest in AI-enabled products, design-led innovation, and operational efficiency. They operate under multiple brands, including Logitech, Jaybird, Logitech G, Blue, Astro, and Ultimate Ears.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Logitech delivered FY25 net sales of $4.55bn, growth of 6% YoY basis and 7% YoY growth on a constant currency basis. The Q4 FY25 revenue was $1.01bn, almost flat on YoY basis but witnessed a ~2% YoY growth on a constant currency basis, which shows stability despite ongoing macro pressures and tariff headwinds.

I believe Logitech’s B2C segment (which is 60%) will remain robust with consistent sell-through momentum led by the company's product superiority and brand reputation. An uptick in demand for more cost-effective entertainment is being provided by the current weakened macro environment, especially in countries such as China, with gaming being at the top of that list. This is being used by management to their advantage in conjunction with proper segmentation, innovation, and partnerships to facilitate demand growth in gaming. B2B demand, especially in video collaboration, continues to benefit from hybrid work adoption. However, enterprise sentiment remains cautious; however, it is noted by management that an opportunity to monetize on the demand for enterprises looking for more cost-effective means of video conferencing is provided by the current environment. The initiative of product superiority in this segment as a demand driver is further reinforced by the addition of AI products with video conferencing. And the company’s recent AI-enabled enterprise video conferencing products (Rally Bar, Rally Board, Sight, etc) are well-positioned for global expansion.

In FY25, Logitech presented a very bold story of innovation and design excellence. 39 new products were launched, delighting users with standout offerings like the Combo Touch Keyboard Case, the Pro X Superlight Gaming Mouse, and China’s top-selling Alto Keys Mechanical Keyboard. Sleek design was combined with software-enabled features in each product, earning global acclaim. This innovation push wasn’t just for show; market share and product categories were expanded by Logitech. A strategy rooted in understanding user needs and constant reinvention reinforced Logitech's identity as a global leader in smart, stylish peripherals.

Market expansion paired with resilient consumer demand should continue to be a tailwind. Logi already holds the #1-2 spot in 11 out of 13 categories where they compete, which shows their brand value. The company is also navigating the tarrif impact strategically, in the mid of April of this year, Logitech implemented a ~10% price increase in US products (from 0% to double-digit percentage increases), which may have some short-term impact, but management believes its strong brand and premium product positioning will support customer acceptance, so price elasticity doesn't look like a major risk here.

All in, I expect LOGI revenue to keep growing, backed by market share gains, steady innovation, and launches. International diversification remains a core revenue driver. With the majority (⅔)of sales outside the U.S., Logitech benefits from global exposure. Logi is witnessing early signs of success in their emerging markets, and I believe that with their deeper channel penetration in EMEA and Asia-Pacific, growth will be further fueled going forward.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Logitech’s profits are holding up well. Logitech's profitability metrics showed strong improvement in FY25. Adj. Gross margin inched up to 43.5% up by 170bps YoY, primarily driven by product cost reductions, value engineering, and better product mix. Adj. Operating income for the same period was $775mn with an operating margin of 17.0% vs 16.3% in FY24.

On the margin front, while tariffs are a headwind, the company is taking several steps to offset them. Gross margins should benefit from continued improvements in their innovation mix and the strategic price increases (~10% avg in the U.S. effective mid-April 2025), early inventory pull-forward, and supply chain optimization.

The company is expecting tariffs to pose a ~$50mn annualized headwind, but they're actively working on ways to soften the blow. Currently, of their total US sales, they source ~40% from China, which they are planning to bring down to merely ~10% by the end of CY25. That shift, along with ongoing cost-saving initiatives and supplier concessions, should help cushion the impact. Still, the tariffs will start hitting Q1 margins before the 2nd round pricing actions kick (the company has indicated the room for increase, but the magnitude and timing are in watch mode).

For the June quarter, management guided the net impact of tariffs to be ~200 bps (including the impact from price increases and cost-saving measures). Looking ahead, recent tariff changes have reduced the gross impact from tariffs from 500 bps to 300 bps, which is expected to be partially offset by ~200 bps of full quarter pricing changes, resulting in a net incremental impact of ~100 bps to gross margins level.

I expect, margin to improve over the medium term, led by positive growth in high-margin categories like video collaboration and AI-powered peripherals. Additionally, positive operating leverage should be provided by disciplined SG&A and ongoing operational efficiencies. The commitment to R&D and innovation was reiterated by management while a tight leash on G&A and discretionary spend is being maintained. Support for gross margin resilience will also be provided by continued value engineering and supply chain optimization.

All in all, I expect the margin to bottom out in Q1 and recover from there onward, with a good medium- to long-term expansion potential, as pricing actions and supply diversification fully materialize.

Peers and Differentiation:

Corsair (CRSR) : While also focusing on gaming peripherals, Corsair positions enthusiast-level products more narrowly. Logitech’s broader product suite and global retail partnerships provide it with greater scale and brand diversification.

HP (HPQ) : Select segments like mice and keyboards are competed in by HP, but Logitech’s dedicated consumer focus and innovation speed are lacking in HP. HP's peripheral business is only a small subset of HP's larger PC and printing ecosystem.

Razer (OTCPK:RAZFF) : Known for gaming hardware, Razer is recognized for its strong design and community engagement, but is operated at a premium pricing tier. Logitech’s strength is found in its ability to balance premium design with accessible pricing, supported by global channel reach.

Key Differentiators:

• Speed to market and innovation cadence (e.g., AI-enabled Rally Bar and Logitech G gaming suite)

• Diversified manufacturing and supply chain footprint

• Strong cash position and operational leverage

• Pricing power supported by brand loyalty

Overall, Logitech’s market positioning is strengthened by its consistent product innovation and supply chain flexibility, distribution scale, allowing market leadership to be maintained even in a fragmented and competitive environment.

Valuation:

Over the period, the company has successfully reduced its dependency on PC to drive its sales of peripherals to a diversified growth company focused on peripherals in gaming, video collaboration, and content creation products. Significant secular drivers underpin the growth of peripherals in these categories (streaming content creation, eSports, hybrid work and learn from home and casual gaming, and increased consumerization of video).

Product innovation remains a key edge. The company’s recent AI-enabled video conferencing products (Rally Bar, Rally Board, Sight, etc) are well received by the market for these high-ASP, higher-margin products.

I believe stock deserve a future re-rating likely on: i) consistent market share gains and ii) likely impact of increased topline in operating leverage. However, while there are some near-term questions related to margin play in, the longer-term worries seem manageable. Management consistently makes an effort to diversify sourcing, and price increases should be absorbed fairly well.

This is in contrast to previous guidance with revenues in the scope of $4.50bn- $4.7bn (or rev growth (CC) of 1%-5%) and operating income of $720- $780mn. While calculating the WACC for RFR, I have used the current US 10-year bond yield of 4.4%. For ERP, I have used Professor Aswath Damodaran's latest data on risk premiums of 4.3%. For beta, I have taken the average of the 5 years and the 2-year company historical beta of 0.9 {(1.15+0.63)/2}, which reflects the balance between the long-term market sensitivity of the business (captured by the 5-year beta) and more recent risk dynamics (captured by the 2-year beta). For perpetuity, the growth rate is considered a 3% rate, reflecting a sustainable growth rate.

Revenue growth numbers (CAGR of 4.8% until FY31e) in the model are based on robust topline growth momentum driven by continued market share expansion, innovation, and growth from core segments like gaming and video collaboration. EBIT (post-tax) margin will go down sequentially in FY26e (mainly due to tariff-related pressure); however, it will gradually pick up in the later years and will return to a normal level. In my DCF calculation, I assumed CapEX to be in line with its historical level, ~1.2 - 1.3% of revenue.

Considering these assumptions, the current fair value for LOGI would be 110.5 and 119.6 (at the end of March 26), or around ~13% and ~23% above the stock's current level.

At 97.4, LOGI trades at ~18.6x FY27e EPS of 5.2 and 17.7x FY28e EPS of 5.5. Past five years, the stock has averaged a forward P/E of 20.3x. So it is evident that it's trading at a discount to its historical average, which reflects its unheard story. If we apply the fwd PE to the EPS of $5.5 FY28e EPS, we will get the fair value of $110.2, signalling undervaluation, a good amount of margin of safety to investors with potential upside surprise after a return to formal FY guidance post-tariff stabilization.

Risks:

Tariff policy volatility and trade disputes - Although the company has taken several steps to diversify its sourcing (significant reduction of supply from China) if it is not able to find suitable partners or the planned price increase is not well accepted by the consumers, this may impact growth.

Supply chain execution or product delays - Despite the company having a strong track record of innovation and distribution, execution risk is always there. Any delay will add further to the risk.

Others - Demand pull-forward risk in peripherals, Geopolitical or economic deterioration in Europe/Asia

Conclusion

I strongly believe that given Logitech's strong fundamentals, supply chain flexibility, and brand strength, they are very well positioned to navigate FY26e uncertainty effectively. Medium-term to Long-term outlook is underpinned by the company's disciplined cost controls, product innovation, and strategic readiness for tariff headwinds. A near-term catalyst could be further fueled by a return to formal FY guidance post-tariff stabilization. I believe the upside is still attractive given the current valuation multiples. Hence, I have a buy rating on LOGI Stock.

data.title: Cracker Barrel's September Earnings Release Could Be Iffy, But I Expect To Remain Bullish
data.description:
Let’s talk biotech, more particularly gene editing, and specifically Intellia Therapeutics (NTLA).

It’s fresh in my mind. I wrote up NTLA on 7/18/25.

Yes, I know this is supposed to be an article on Cracker Barrel Old Country (NASDAQ:CBRL). And it will be.

But investment themes can and often do transcend differences between companies, industries, etc.

Both situations involve how we should react to bad news.

As soon as supposedly negative developments surface, hot takes typically dictate selling the stocks. Prices drop. Analyst ratings often do likewise.

Anger and frustrations rise.

But eventually, good analysis take over.

That happened with NTLA.

The stock plummeted after news surfaced on 5/29/25 about a safety concern involving a pioneering treatment in Phase III FDA testing.

But then, as investors took more time to study up, it became apparent that the hot takes were wrong. The NTLA investment case remained intact. That stock now trades above where it did before the supposed bad news surfaced.

Unfortunately, the dramatic NTLA headline came so suddenly, the best I could do was write about it after the fact.

CBRL is different. The potential issues I envision relate to ordinary economic factors. That gives me an opportunity to alert you to their potential occurrence ahead of time.

The major CBRL issue, the one that motivated my previous “Buy” recommendations, is company-specific and secular.

It’s about a five-point transformation plan launched by Julie Masino after she became CEO on 11/1/23.

I’ll discuss the plan below. For now, I want to highlight the fact that CBRL badly needed something like this.

My first exposure to CBRL came in the 1990s. Back then, I covered restaurant stocks at Value Line - including CBRL. The company struck me as an intriguing oddball. I’d not (and still haven’t) seen any of their restaurants. That’s because they don’t operate near New York City, where I live.

Rural “country” charm is a big part of its brand. It locates near exits of major highways. And it strategically catered to folks who traveled by car. In fact, my contact back in the day (I can’t recall if his function was Finance or Investor Relations) told me some customers would plan travel routes, even vacations, around places where CBRL was located.

As an arrogant New Yorker, the CBRL brand did not grab me. I was way too hip for that sort of thing.

But CBRL knew its customer base. And the company spoke clearly and effectively to those who it believed would like its brand.

That’s fine. I’m a stock analyst, not a food critic or judge. So I assessed CBRL based on what it aimed to be. My comments had a favorable slant. Still…

The stock wasn’t a barnburner. As the ‘90s progressed, there was no way a stock like CBRL could outrun what we now know to have been the build-up to what eventually became the dot-com bubble.

But CBRL’s fundamentals were fine.

I summarize some key statistics in the table below. But I’ll warn you ahead of time… CBRL didn’t maintain its strength. By the fiscal year that ended 7/31/24, things had deteriorated badly.

We see that:

  • Return on Equity (ROE) badly deteriorated. Note, though that heavy debt boosts ROE (profits are divided by a smaller equity base). CBRL’s ROE deterioration comes even though…
  • The debt-to-equity ratio soared.
  • Return on Assets (ROA), where it doesn’t matter how much or how little debt a company has, collapsed horrendously.
  • The dividend payout ratio and dividend yield went from next to nothing (consistent with the idea of CBRL as a growth stock) to much higher levels. Indeed, CBRL became known as an income stock… until the company eventually recognized that the payout ratio had become unsustainable given CBRL's need to invest in its restaurants. The dividend was slashed on 5/16/24. So the income angle vaporized. It’s now growth or bust.

The pandemic certainly didn’t help CBRL. But its recent difficulties go beyond that. To put it as politely as possible, the brand lost relevance to the company’s target market.

It wasn’t any one big thing. It was a lot of little things that added up to a brand that became tired, or stale.

Here’s an update on CBRL’s current core numbers.

Let's start with the quarterly Revenue and EPS trends.

The EPS table really gives you a sense of how far CBRL now has to go!

Let's move to the historic and projected forward growth rates.

(I compare CBRL to medians among companies in the Restaurant industry and in the portfolio of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). Because CBRL is small, I also compare it to the SPDR Portfolio S&P 600 Small Cap ETF (SPSM). I use medians since these aren’t impacted by wild distortions often caused by unusual data items.)

That’s nothing to write home about.

Here are the core fundamentals.

It’s not the worst I’ve ever seen. But I wouldn’t call it a magnet that attracts investors to a stock.

The debt-related numbers remain noticeably high.

And here are the financial statement highlights.

We see a little sense of the unfortunate path suggested in the above 1990s-versus-7/24 table.

Hence the need for Masino’s hiring. And hence the need for the five-part transformation program she launched.

I discussed Masino’s credentials and introduced her transition program on 6/7/24, when I first wrote on CBRL for Seeking Alpha. I said “Buy.” I reiterated the “Buy” rating on 12/10/24.

The stock barely moved between my first two reports. It rose only 2%. Then it hit a pothole. At one point after my second writeup, the stock was down about 25%. It soon recovered… and then some

As of now, it’s up about 44%, since the 6/7/24 writeup. The S&P 500 is up about 5%.

Keep this roller coaster in mind

Given the stock’s recent strength, we’d like to think it’ll be a great journey from here.

But as good as the stock’s been lately…

Don’t Complacently Expect a Turbulence-Free Trip Forward

We’re now aiming at CBRL’s next (9/18/25) earning-report date.

Maybe it’ll be good. If we could rely on company-specific factors alone, I’d probably say just that.

Unfortunately for CBRL, all companies, and all of us, that’s not the world in which we live. We’re all vulnerable to economic and political forces outside our control.

We see that to some degree in the following table showing CBRL’s segment highlights:

There are good things here.

For now, however, notice CBRL’s Comparable Restaurant Guest Traffic declines. Those numbers are bad.

Here’s what CBRL said about them on page 22 of its latest 10Q:

The decreases in guest traffic are primarily the result of lower consumer demand arising from multiple macroeconomic factors, including inflationary pressures, higher consumer debt levels and lower savings rates as well as the potential uncertainty associated with the geopolitical environment and global trade.

To anybody who has been following the news, that sounds very credible.

I still can’t tell you what our tariff policies are because even if I get it right for this minute, it could be very different by tomorrow. We’ve all seen how changeable everything has been.

CBRL is trying its best to address tariffs by sourcing imported products from other places. But that’s imperfect. It’s also having to adjust pricing.

And then, too, there’s the impact on CBRL’s customers. How many of them are feeling the pinch employment- or income-wise as tariffs impact their employers or their own businesses.

CBRL has not as of this writing adjusted forward guidance.

On 6/5/25, it reported favorable comparable store sales four quarters in a row. That’s great… and testament to the success so far of Masino’s efforts.

But considering the various forms of squeeze impacting CBRL and restaurants in general, be alert. There’s just so much economic pressure CBRL can withstand before it impacts more numbers.

But as cautious as I am, I’m staying at “Buy.”

This is where the NTLA story I told on 7/18/25 will come into play…

Even if Factors Beyond CBRL’s Control Hit Its Stock, Focus on the Primary Company-Specific Investment Issue: the Five Part Transition Plan

NTLA shares got hammered, but soon more than recovered after the Street come to understand a supposed safety issue was in fact medically meaningless.

A similar scenario might play out with CBRL after it reports results.

I cannot and would not dare to suggest this far ahead of the fact that any earnings- or guidance-related disappointment would be meaningless. It was the same with NTLA’s issue. We cannot judge in advance.

But after the information becomes available, we must evaluate thoroughly and carefully. This sort of thing needs time. Hot takes won’t suffice.

Sure, we’d be underwater on our stock position. We can’t help that. The market reacts almost instantaneously. But if we’re right, we’ll be rewarded as the stock recovers its loss, and possibly more depending on the situation.

The issue for CBRL that will require patient thought and analysis will be whether the transition program will still look like it’s working.

The following images from a 1/13/25 CBRL presentation summarize the plan:

Refreshing the brand is a big part of what CBRL is looking to do. An important part of this relates to the look and feel of the units.

So let’s start with a visual summary of the brand.

Look back at the image at the beginning of this article. Next, look at the below image.

What does this convey to you?

I think most observers would use phrases like homey, comfortable, friendly, relaxed, etc.

It’s a far cry from, say, often sleek omakase sushi establishments! It’s a massive step above fast food - oops, I think today I’m supposed to say “quick serve.”

Compare it to places like Applebee’s, or Cheesecake Factory (CAKE). We’re getting closer to CBRL. But really think about this. (I suggest going to Google to find more CBRL images.)

Wouldn’t you agree that CBRL stands out from the almost generic looks of other casual eateries. I’m getting more subjective here. But that’s what makes branding and brand refresh so darn hard.

We like phrases such as homey, friendly, relaxed, etc. But we hate words labels like stale, old fashioned, has-been, etc.

Management’s delicate task is to eliminate the latter group of phrases while retaining the former.

This is a year-one of a multi-year program. Masino described this on the 6/5/25 earnings call as a “test and learn investment year.”

That’s what we should be looking for when CBRL reports its next set of results.

I obviously don’t know what Masino will discuss on the next earnings call. But I can set the table by reviewing some of what CBRL has accomplished so far in this, its test and learn year.

Early Transitional Program Developments

NOTE: All management quotes from CEO Julie Masino and CFO Craig Pommells come from the transcript of the 6/5/25 earnings call.

Here, now, are the high points of management's update.

Look and Feel: The Brand

I alluded to this above - the challenge of updating the chain’s appearance and ambiance without losing that which served the company well in the past.

Here’s what Masino said:

As we've discussed, this has really been a year of testing and learning. And so we are saving kind of this topic for September. So we will talk a lot about it in September, really what we've learned in this year and what we continue to learn… [W[e've got 20 remodels and 20 refreshes that as of right now are complete in the system. And we continue to be really pleased with what we're learning there… employees have given us great feedback about working in those newly remodeled and refreshed stores and guests continue to tell us that they're lighter, brighter more welcoming and they're enjoying them as well….

[A]t the end of April, we launched a new version of a remodel as well. And so it's early, early days of that.

Bottom line: It’s still early days here. We’ll look to hear much more on the September call.

Marketing

CBRL seems to be one a roll here. The following, from Masino, was an eye opener for a once sleepy company that seemed (to me) deeply tied to the past.

[W]e are showing up authentically in places where our existing and new guests are.

An example of this is our partnership with Speedway Motorsports and the success of the Cracker Barrel 400…. There's strong overlap with Cracker Barrel guests and NASCAR fans, and our brands have much in common. Both are highly experiential and put country hospitality and people at the heart of everything we do. The Cracker Barrel 400 is more than a race. It marks the launch of a key partnership….

This branding initiative extends to the menu.

it is also a piece of our overall strategy and integrated marketing campaign to promote the much anticipated return of Campfire meals.

We heard loud and clear for both guests and employees that they deeply missed these unique and delicious foil-wrapped meals that are packed with hearty proteins, seasoned vegetables and a rich broth. We brought them back for the first time since 2018 and made them even better. We've elevated the flavors, improved the quality and made them easier for the kitchen to execute.

In addition to the returning favorites of beef and chicken, we've added a new shrimp and endue sausage offering starting at the great value price point of $10.99.

I’ll show you what that looks like in the next section. For now, just contemplate how intriguing a departure this is from the company’s formerly sleepy (as it seemed to me) past.

CBRL is also working hard to develop a loyalty program. Masino said:

Cracker Barrel Rewards is another way we're deepening our engagement with guests and driving frequency….

We recently achieved our fiscal '25 year target of acquiring 8 million members and over 1/3 of track sales are now associated with loyalty members.

The less-discussed smaller retail part of the business is also under the microscope. According to Masino:

One of the key tenets of what we're looking at from a retail strategy is the number of SKUs that we have, the number of themes that we have and the timing of when they hit the floor. We've been known to put Christmas and Halloween out quite early. And so we're readjusting some of that timing to really be more in time with where consumer need state and demand is.

And so we've got a lot of moving pieces while this tariff thing is coming in. So the teams have been really working for a while now on rationalizing SKUs, thinking about those themes, thinking about the timing and moving all of those pieces.

A lot of moving pieces. Oh my God! Google to find pictures of CBRL stores. There sure are plenty of moving pieces. I have to believe there’s upside to any plausible SKU rationalization effort.

  • Menu

No matter how many things executives and investment analysts discuss, let’s remember that ultimately, restaurants are about food.

And it can’t be just any food, even well-cooked good tasting food. It has to be about the kind of food its customers want.

The CBRL menu is not necessarily what I’d want. But I’m a New Yorker living in the one of the most ethnically diverse neighborhoods in the U.S.

CBRL is likely right to not operate near me. I’m addicted to Asian cuisines, and I love South American pastries.

Or maybe I'm being too narrow minded.

Would my neighbors view the CBRL menu as an intriguing novelty – something different from what they’re used to. Ultimately, though, I’ll leave that choice to CBRL management!

When you contemplate CBRL’s menu, whether from the illustrations I’ll show or from other things you can discover via the company’s on-line menu (it’ll ask you to pick a location; if nothing is near you, just pick anything in the South or Midwest), consider, the target customers… such as folks who’d resonate with NASCAR, folks who live in generally rural areas. If they’re happy, CBRL shareholders will eventually become happy.

Let’s start with the above-mentioned campfire meals:

Personally, I want nothing to do with campfires. To me nature is best perceived through IMAX or the window of an air conditioned car. But it’s easy for me to imagine CBRL’s target customers loving this.

Here’s the Louisiana Style Shrimp Skillet:

OK. I’m not from the South. But shrimp with a culinary kick - I’m in.

Here are some more samples:

Sign me up for the Fresh Berry Salad, and for a change of pace, the Sausage & Egg Hashbrown Casserole. I love the items in the latter dish individually. I never tried, but would be open to checking them out, as a unified offering.

Now it’s time I become a bad person:

I want those! I want them NOW!

But I wouldn’t want my wife, or my doctor, to see me enjoying them. I call these YOLO (you-only-live-once) offerings.

Actually, even though I’m light years away from CBRL’s targeted customer universe, there’s stuff here that intrigues me. If one of the Food Network personalities that go out into the field to film restaurant visits and does a feature on a CBRL, I’ll watch… and drool.

As long as the food tastes good, it’s hard to deny that CBRL’s menu is a conversation piece. That it’s so non-generic is one of the reasons I think this company can succeed - as long as everything else is done well.

Pricing

Here’s what Pommells said about pricing:

[O]ur pricing guidance is -- we're providing annual guidance in that regard, and it's essentially unchanged from what we said before, which is approximately 5%. And then in terms of the overall check dynamic, the check was up 6.6% for quarter. That includes 4.9% of pricing, 1.7% of mix. So a couple of encouraging things there. On past calls, we've talked a lot about the barbell pricing strategy and just kind of the data-driven approach to pricing.

CBRL’s ability to offset declining customer traffic with check-size gains says a lot about progress on this front. (See Operating Segments table above.)

Operations

Masino’s goal here is to make it easier and more pleasant for employees to do their jobs.

And, of course, she aims to do this without hurting the company’s cost structure. (So maybe avoiding expensive New York City is right even if CBRL could interest people ere in its menu!)

Here’s how she describes where these efforts are and where they’re heading:

The first phase is really focused on some of those processes and changing the way that we actually make the food to improve the quality and make the jobs easier. So that's kind of Phase 1. Phase 2 is about how do we take that even further by bringing in some ingredients that are already like pre-chopped and presliced and things like that because today, we do all of that by hand or most of it by hand.

And then Phase 3 is, gosh, equipment has changed so much in the last few decades. Are there equipment solutions that would also make it easier for our cooks and our prep cooks to do their work easier. So those 3 phases will phase out over the remaining years of the transformation.

Let’s keep watching. We’ll need more time to fully assess things. But on paper, it looks like a sound plan.

Summary

It looks to me like CBRL had a very productive first year of investing and learning. The company appears to be on the right track.

Risk

This isn’t biotech. This isn’t AI. This isn't quantum computing. This isn’t crypto. So I hesitate to say, for CBRL, that risk is high.

Let me put it this way: Risk is high by consumer sector and restaurant industry standards.

Brand refresh, especially the delicate balance between the new and old that CBRL is trying to navigate, is hard. The risk is that the company will fall shy here of expectations.

Survival risk here is not something over which one should lose sleep. The bad scenario would be that CBRL turns into a long-term industry also-ran… and that the stock comes to reflect this.

What to do About CBRL Stock

From what I see so far, I think the transition program will succeed. How much success might it have?

I’ll think the company will do well enough to make its present valuations look lean in retrospect.

I have to tell you that the 3- to 5-year EPS growth number I saw on Seeking Alpha, 0.8% as of this writing, looks bizarre.

Either the data provider is using, as a starting point, a high historical number. (This happens a lot.) If that’s the case, I disagree. I think the latest set of results makes for the best base. That’s because it would match up with today’s stock price.

The other possibility is that analysts are factoring in nothing, or very little, from the transition program. If that’s so, I disagree given my assessment of the credibility of the program and the early progress the company is making.

To create the above table, I eliminated the database’s 0.8% expected growth rate. Instead, I plugged in the 10.96% industry median. Absent bad news on the transition plan, I think that’s a reasonable assumption.

Based on that, the PEG ratio looks relatively attractive.

So, too, does the EV/S ratio, especially if, as I assume, the company can expend its margins over time.

Here’s the price chart.

That’s definitely a bullish chart.

The price trend looks strong. So, too, do trends in the 10- and 50-day exponential moving averages (EMAs).

So, too, do the Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and Chaikin Oscillator (CO) indicators. Both measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

Both suggest buyers are more motivated than sellers. That exerts upward pressure on stock prices.

The question, though, is whether the company’s September earnings release and/or guidance will diminish any of the enthusiasm.

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I’m not psychic. I can’ tell you what the company will report in September. So near term, anything can happen to CBRL stock.

But based on secular factors (relating to Masino’s transition program), I see CBRL as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market.

If CBRL takes a bad detour in response to September results and/or guidance, I’ll stick with my view… so long as the company continues to progress in its transition. (NTLA recovered from its hot-take hit. I would expect likewise from CBRL if it comes to that.)

I translate all this to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by continuing to rate CBRL as a “Buy.”

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Undervalued Despite Strong Growth Trend & Solid Market Share
data.description:
Thesis statement

I am bullish on Novo Nordisk A/S (NYSE: NVO), driven by its 62% market share (globally) in the GLP-1 market. In this market leadership, NVO's Ozempic dominates GLP-1 sales by 34.17% as of the end of 2024, which explains why the segment has a high potential to maintain the lead in the GLP-1 market. In my view, it is a good leverage point to seize the GLP-1 receptor market, which is estimated to grow at a CAGR of 17.46% between 2025 and 2030.

In my bullish outlook, I believe NVO has a better position in the obesity and type 2 diabetes market because, even as a new anti-obesity pill known as Mounjaro or Orforglibron, threatens its competitiveness, NVO's Rybelsus pill is already in the market treating type 2 diabetes and could, in future, be approved to treat obesity. Rybelsus also reduces cardiovascular complications by 14% after four years of its use, which introduces a new treatment option for people living with diabetes and heart disease. The Ozempic, which is injectable, reduces the risk of cardiac arrest and death by 24% making it a preferred drug for patients diagnosed with type-2 diabetes. These levels of treatment explain why NVO's option, such as Ozempic, has the largest market share at 62% while competitors share the remaining percentage in the treatment of type 2 diabetes.

With this brief overview of my bullish angle, I will now delve into a broader and more detailed rationale for why I think its larger market share is a major driver informing my bullish stance.

Company overview

Novo Nordisk A/S is an international healthcare company focused on research and development, manufacturing, and commercialization of pharmaceutical products. The company operates two segments: Diabetes and Obesity, and Rare Disease. Under the Diabetes and Obesity care segment, the company offers products such as insulin, GLP-1 therapies, oral antidiabetic medications, and treatments for obesity, glycogen, and other long-term conditions. The Rare Disease segment provides treatment solutions for endocrine issues, blood disorders, growth disorders, and hormone replacement therapy. Under the partnership with Gilead Sciences, Novo Nordisk A/S is researching cell-specific carriers for nucleic acid therapies. The company was founded in 1923 and is headquartered in Bagsvaerd, Denmark.

A broader perspective of the GLP-1 market

From a broader perspective, the GLP-1 market is estimated to grow at a CAGR of 17.46% between 2025 and 2030, reaching a market volume of $156.71 billion.

Let’s take America as an example where the number of Americans taking GLP-1 is currently around 8% to 10% while 30% to 35% have no access to GLP-1 but are interested in it.

Individuals with access and those without access to GLP-1 for weight loss medication demand GLP-1 at a rate of 60%, and for individuals with diabetes are demanding GLP-1 at a rate of 58%. Despite GLP-1 remaining among the most expensive drugs for many Americans as shown in the image below, pills alternatives are about to close this gap. This illustrates how the demand is high without a matching supply at affordable prices.

This gives a hint as to why there are over 550 million patients globally living with diabetes and have unmet medication needs. But let's see where NVO has positioned itself in this market gap.

My bullish view

With a global GLP-1 market share of 62%, this, in my view, gives NVO an edge over its competitors such as Eli Lily, which has a market share of 35%. Despite the first quarter of 2025, NVO scaling down of its sales growth outlook for FY 2025 from 16% to 24% to between 13% to 21%, you might ask yourself why I am still bullish. While this was a cautious decision following competition, I still find it attractive at double digit with a massive market opportunity to sustain it.

With more than 50% of the US population estimated to be obese by 2030, I see a significant opportunity for GLP-1 weight loss medication.

For example, 65% of providers are willing to prescribe GLP-1 to patients. As such, this fuels an uptrend for GLP-1 specifically for weight loss in 2025 and beyond as continued utilization reveal positive results in terms reducing the weight.

While weight loss shows a huge potential for GLP-1 usefulness amid rising competition, diabetes offers another opportunity. With more than 38 million Americans living with diabetes and approximately 1.2 million new cases each year, this company has another customer segment it can leverage for its growth. I see this opportunity through the lens of obesity. How I see it is in the fact that 90% of people diagnosed with type 2 diabetes are affected by a degree of obesity, and they are overweight. In America, where 41.9% of the population is obese, NOV leads the GPL-1 market share by 52.3% and it has maintained a growth this market share over years.

Given the market leadership of NOV in terms of global GLP-1 medicine sales, Ozempic holds the largest share as of 2025, at 37.3%. This is a market reach advantage for NOV's Ozempic medication, giving it the capability to sell its Rybelsus, which will counter its competitive alternatives, such as Orforglipron by Eli Lily, a pill alternative for Ozempic. The Rybelsus pill is an option for Ozempic, and it faces the Orforglipron pill as a close alternative pill for type 2 diabetes. Novo is ahead with the approval of Rybelsus, which is the treatment approval for type 2 diabetes. This market leadership gives NOV an edge in Rybelsus easier market entry ahead of the Orforglipron pill. Orforglipron pills are expected to be approved by 2026, which, in my opinion, allows NOV to market its Rybelsus with less competitiveness. On the other hand, Wegovy, an injectable medication for obesity, has a pill version pending FDA approval and is expected to submit a New Drug Application at the end of Q4'2025.

When I consider this potential market outlook, the pill options NOV is offering are a counter strategy to maintain its leading market share by diversifying its product range to appeal to more diverse customer needs as well as target more conditions. I believe this will offset the effects of the current competitve pressure and help in sustaining its growth in the mid 20s atleast in the short and midterms.

Financials: Q2 2025 Expectations And What To Watch

Before I get to my Q2 expectations, I will reflect on the company’s past performance both the top and bottom line. From both an annual and quarterly perspective, NVO has been consistently reporting double digit growth in its revenue. Annually, the company has seen its revenue growth ranging between 10.91% to 31.25% since 2021, with its growth seemingly stabilizing in the mid 20% range.

The same trend is seen its quarterly revenue growth ranging between 24% and 37% since Q1 2022. Mostly recently, other than Q4 2024 when the growth was about 30%, the average quarterly growth rate ranges at about 22%.

In my view, this consistency is a reflection of the strong demand for its products which I believe stems from its solid market share discussed previously. Secondly, its consistency is a reflection of its strong commercial execution as shown by 30% growth in North America operations and 19% international operations growth in 2024 FY.

Moving to its profitability, the company has consistently maintained profitability, with its financials on an overall uptrend.

For The YoY net income growth, for more than 5 years, the company has maintained a positive growth figures.

Its net income growth has been turbulent recently going as low as 3.2% in Q2 2024 before rising to double digit growth. This could be explained by several factors, such as currency fluctuation and tax R&D and SG&A spending surges. For instance, in 2024, R&D expenses went up by about 48% compressing profits.

With this background, here follows my estimates (expectations) for the upcoming quarter. I am estimating a revenue of about $12.21 billion, which is a 25% YoY growth and consistent with its double digit revenue growth in the region of mid 20s. This will be slightly above the consensus estimates of $11.95 billion. For the EPS, I am estimating an EPS of $0.96, marking a YoY growth of 45%. This will be slightly above the projected EPS of $0.94. I believe this will be fueled by the growth in revenue at a time when the company's total expenses are dipping, a sign of improved cost efficiency.

In a nutshell, I expect a strong Q2 2025 with both revenue and EPS surprises driven by strong GLP-1 product momentum and improved cost management.

Besides the financial metrics, other key areas of focus for investors are pipeline updates and manufacturing capacity expansion. I strongly believe that this company’s long term viability relies heavily on the success of its next generation therapies in the obesity, cardiovascular and diabetes diseases. As such, trails like REDEFINE 1 and STEP-UP can reshape market share and treatment paradigms. Therefore, I believe any positive developments here could trigger market optimisms. For this reason, investors should watch out for any data readouts, or trial enrollment. Further, we should set our sight on any potential expansion into new indications or regulatory feedback or submission timelines.

Secondly, as the demand for GLP-1 remains high as discussed earlier, NVO’s ability to scale production is critical. Any signs of delays, challenges or underutilization could constrain revenue despite strong demand. Consequently, we should watch for backlog resolution or lead time metrics for Ozempic and Wegovy as well as any capacity ramp up at any facility.

Valuation

To value this stock, I will use its P/S ratio and compare is with that of its competitor Eli Lilly(LLY). NVO is currently trading at a P/S of 7.07x, which is more than 51% lower than its counterpart’s 14.67x.

This clearly shows that NVO is trading at a significantly discount. Although LLY has a higher YoY revenue growth rate of 36.38%, compared to NVO’s 24.11%, I think its massive under valuation is not justified. With an approximately 33% lower revenue growth than LLY, I think the justified P/S would be about 9.8x which would be approximately 67% of LLY’s 14.67x. Assuming this P/S, the trailing revenue of $43,940.7 million and the 4457diluted weighted average shares outstanding I estimate a fair value of $96.62. This marks an upside potential of about 37.97%. Given this company’s massive market share and strong demand for its products, I think this is possible and I rate it a buy. My estimate is within range with 5 wall Street analysts whose consensus price target is $86.50 marking a 29.53% upside.

Investment risks

Regulatory changes – There is a risk of regulatory changes that could lead to a change in GLP-1 pricing by the current US government, which is seeking reduced drug prices. This may interfere with NOV revenues if the pricing cap fails to align with production costs.

Competition- Despite NOV commanding a larger market share globally, there is a risk of new entrants and varied GPL-1 medications that could erode its market share.

Change in people's preferences – Individuals who are currently using medication may change their need for medicines to other options, such as observing a diet and exercise regimen.

Conclusion

The company is undervalued given its larger market share and new drug competitiveness that solidify its market leadership. For this reason, I am reiterating my bullish stance.
