data.title: Tesla's 'iPhone' Moment Is Here
data.description:
I discussed why I increased my Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) position around the lows in March and April, as well as on the massive early June drop when CEO Elon Musk's Twitter storm erupted, which included the now infamous and since deleted "Jeffrey Epstein Tweet."

Now, you might be wondering why I am accumulating Tesla's stock when the company's CEO exhibits what could arguably be characterized as erratic behavior.

Well, generally, I feel that Tesla's stock hit a long-term bottom around the $220-200 support range, and due to numerous constructive fundamental factors like its market leading position in EVs, full self-driving ("FSD"), robotaxis, AI, robotics, energy, and more, Tesla's stock could have enormous upside ahead.

Do you remember when all those Teslas were burning? Well, due to the ultra-bearish sentiment and other transitory factors, that unprecedented chaotic period very likely coincided with the bottom in Tesla's stock, in my opinion.

Now, technically, Tesla's stock is in a very interesting place, as it appears to be forming a long term W-shaped bottom. We can also view this image as a bullish long term inverse head and shoulders set-up. Moreover, we have the 50-day MA crossing over the 200-day MA recently. This dynamic illustrates a bullish golden-cross, which is also suggestive of a long term bottoming process, which supports the likelihood of higher price action, especially in the intermediate and long term.

While Tesla's stock could pull back to about the $300-280 support range, it is not likely to fall beyond that unless we have a considerable broad market correction. In this "bearish case scenario," we could witness Tesla's stock test around the $270-250 support range.

Tesla's Q2 Earnings Beat Estimates

Tesla reported revenue of $22.49B, beating the consensus figure by $360M. Normalized EPS came in at $0.40, which was inline with the consensus estimate. My estimate was for $22.65B in sales and adjusted EPS of $0.44.

While Tesla's automotive segment produced about the sales I anticipated, yet its operating costs were a bit higher than I thought. For instance, I thought that Tesla's automotive sales revenues would be about $15.6B (without tax credits), and Tesla delivered about $15.8B. The company also utilized less tax credit revenue than I anticipated, $439M vs. my estimate of $700M, which is a positive sign.

This dynamic may illustrate that Tesla is slowly winding down its partial dependence on tax credit revenues. Therefore, it should remain profitable once the EV tax credits are phased out, and it could increase its profitability by generating more revenues from higher margin businesses in the coming years.

Also, I believe that Tesla could regain more momentum auto sales wise, and could return to considerable growth in its automotive segment early next year. Moreover, Tesla reaffirmed that it's on track to introduce its new lower cost EV in H2 2025. The new vehicle could contribute considerable revenues to Tesla's top line next year, enabling it to surpass future revenues and ESP estimates.

It's Not All About Selling Cars Anymore

Services and other revenues expanded nicely to about $3.05B, about a 16% QoQ rise. This was above my $2.9B estimate. However, Tesla's energy generation and storage "EG&S" business' sales only rose slightly over its previous quarter.

The EG&S segment brought in about $2.79B in sales, slightly lower than my $3B estimate. Due to the White House's harsh stance on solar and renewable energy in general (aside from nuclear), it's a challenging time for the entire alt-energy industry now.

Therefore, I am not surprised that Tesla's EG&S business isn't growing as fast now as some would like. Nonetheless, Tesla's energy segment has enormous potential going forward, and this business alone could be worth around $200B.

Tesla's Profitability Better Than Expected

Tesla produced a gross margin of 17.2% in Q2, which was a considerable improvement over last Q's 16.3%, and slightly above my 17% estimate. If we exclude the tax credit revenue Tesla's gross margin was 15.6%, still relatively high, especially for a "car company."

For instance, Ford's (F) recent gross margins have been around 8%, while GM's (GM) roughly 12%. Moreover, Tesla's automotive sales segment gross margin (not including tax credits) was 17.2%, illustrating superior manufacturing efficiency over its competitors. In addition, Tesla also posted a $284M gain on its Bitcoin (BTC-USD) holdings, which atypical for a car company.

It's All About The Ecosystem And The Future Now

People often ask me "what is Tesla's FSD advantage?" "Why should it be the one to succeed and potentially 'corner the robotaxi market' in the future, and why not Waymo, or someone else?"

Well, it's because no-one has the ecosystem or the technological advantage that Tesla does, in my view, not even Alphabet (GOOG, GOOGL), when it comes to self-driving cars.

First, Tesla uses its own proprietary technology, and not lidar detectors like most other companies utilize. While the system is far from perfect yet, I believe Tesla will iron out the kinks in time.

Tesla can utilize higher resolution cameras, improve neuralink capabilities, incorporate additional sensors, improve AI capabilities, and much more. Knowing Tesla, there is a very high probability that the company will be able to figure out solutions to its transitory FSD issues.

Therefore, it could become the number one player in this ultra-lucrative and highly profitable robotaxi/FSD space. Even Goldman Sachs thinks that Tesla has a significant "leg up," so I am not the only one.

Here's How I View Tesla And It's Not A "Car Stock"

Think about it this way. Each Tesla vehicle is actually a robot on wheels. There could be about 5 million active Tesla vehicles globally now, and each one of them has numerous cameras. These cameras continuously collect and transmit data back to Tesla's enormous data center AI system, and its supercomputer Dojo.

Tesla is also building thousands of robots this year, and it may produce around 50,000 units next year. I don't know about you, but I view this as a "iPhone" moment, similar to the one I had in 2006. When I saw Apple coming out with a superior, market-leading revolutionary product (the iPhone), I just knew that in several years essentially everyone would have one. I bought Apple's stock for the long term in 2006, and the rest is history.

I have a similar feeling regarding Tesla's Optimus robot. It seems so much more refined than anything the competition has to offer, in my view. Moreover, it looks amazing in my opinion, as many "competing" robots have wires and other aesthetic issues. In other words, the Optimus bot reminds me very much of the iPhone in 2006.

Robots Could Be A Massive Business For Tesla

I understand that the robot is a much more expensive product, but that doesn't mean that between now and 2030 many families and business won't own them. If Tesla sells only about 100,000 bots in a year for $25,000 each it would equate to about $2.5B in revenues.

Elon Musk believes that the humanoid robot segment will grow into a multi-trillion-dollar business, and Tesla could dominate this sector as well. While Elon has been a little optimistic about some timelines, I've learned not to doubt the man too much, especially when it comes to highly capable and applicable future technologies, like robots.

Tesla aims to produce about 1M robots a year by 2030. Therefore, if we approximate an average selling price of $25K for each robot, this segment could bring in an additional $25B annually for Tesla's top line.

Short Selling Risks To Consider

While I have nothing against shorting stocks for short-term profits, Tesla is not a stock I would short in the intermediate and long term. I've never thought it a wise idea to short companies that are involved in developing and implementing revolutionary products and technologies.

So, I just think that anyone who is shorting Tesla longer term with put options or any other instruments because of its seemingly high valuation could be making a mistake. I only mention this because I don't want to see more people lose even more money betting against Tesla, as I've been witnessing this dynamic for over ten years now.

Where Tesla's Stock Could Go Longer Term

Year 2025 2026 2027 2028 2029 2030
Revenue (in billions) $112 $138 $173 $220 $270 $325
Revenue growth 15% 23% 25% 27% 23% 20%
EPS $2.50 $4.70 $6.25 $9.70 $14 $19
EPS growth 3.3% 88% 33% 55% 44% 36%
Forward P/E

110

 100 95 85 75 65
Stock price $525 $625 $920 $1,190

$1,425

 $1,625

Source: The Financial Prophet.

I am keeping my intermediate and longer term estimates the same as in my previous analysis. I see no need to change them at this point. However, I could adjust estimates higher as more clarity materializes concerning Tesla's new vehicle, robotaxi and FSD magnetization growth, and potential improvements in general efficiencies and automotive sales.

Therefore, these are my lower/base case scenario projections. In a more bullish outcome, Tesla could achieve market dominance in FSD, robotaxis, EVs, robotics, successfully integrating many of the moving parts into its highly advanced ecosystem. In such a scenario, and I believe it is highly plausible, Tesla's stock could go much higher, and my 2030 price target range remains $2,500-3,000 for Tesla's stock.

Risks To My Bullish Thesis

Of course, there are many risks to the bull case in Tesla, which could be why there are so many bearish views about the stock. Tesla faces increased competition in the EV space, and its sales have slumped due to political involvement by CEO Elon Musk.

Also, there is the risk from the current administration possibly pressuring Tesla because of the ruptured relationship between President Trump and Elon Musk. The EV tax credits are being phased out, and there is still plenty of uncertainty regarding Tesla's Cybertaxi program and the safety and full-scale functionality of its FSD. The risks as well as the potential rewards are abundant, and investors should examine these and other risks carefully before purchasing Tesla's stock.

data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Buffett Drops The Mic
data.description:
Introduction

Buffett's 60 years at the helm of Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.B) is coming to an end. His performance is staggering: a 19.9% average annual return over 60 years (up to the 2024 year-end). If you calculate the performance until the annual meeting on May 3rd, when he announced that Greg Abel will succeed him, the yearly return is 20.2%! Although I believe Buffett is the greatest investor of all time, I think it is wise of him to step down at the top and let the next generation take over. At 94 years old, it's no shame to admit that your best days are behind you. And who knows, maybe Warren will be managing his personal portfolio full time when he retires due to his love for the sport. Actually, that's probably the most likely scenario.

This is a rundown of Berkshire Hathaway's long-term performance, updated annually. I review the figures in the same way that Buffett has historically and provide a valuation framework for Berkshire Hathaway. Without further ado, let's dig into the numbers.

Yardsticks

Berkshire can be viewed from a few different lenses. A fairly simplistic one is to think of Berkshire as having two separate segments of value.

The first is investments in stocks, bonds and cash equivalents which stood at $574 billion at year-end ($488 billion last year) and $581 billion at the end of Q1 2025. Float has funded about $173 billion of these investments.

The second source of value comes from Berkshire's over 60 non-insurance businesses. In their early days, Charlie and Warren focused on increasing the value of the investment portfolio (per-share investment). However, since the late 1980s, they focused more on developing earnings from non-insurance operations (per-share pre-tax earnings). You can also call this bucket operating company earnings. Earnings from insurance companies and investment income are not included in this area as they are implicitly included in the first bucket.

This shift in focus on operating companies accelerated the growth rate of this bucket, while the growth rate of the investment bucket slowed. However, from 2007 onwards, both buckets have slowed down considerably.

This can be seen from the following tables:

Year Per-share investments Years

Compounded annual gain in per-share investments
1965 4
1979 577 1965-1979 42.8%
1993 13,961 1979-1993 25.6%
2007 90,343 1993-2007 14.3%
2021 303,563 2007-2021 9.0%
2024 399,240 2021-2024 9.6%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2007 shareholder letter and financial statements

Per-share investments have increased by 21.1% compounded annually over the entire period from 1965 to 2024 (60 years for those who are counting).

Year Per-share pre-tax earnings Years Compounded annual gain in per-share pre-tax earnings
1965 4
1979 18 1965-1979 11.1%
1993 212 1979-1993 19.1%
2007 4,093 1993-2007 23.5%
2021 17,696 2007-2021 11.0%
2024 19,271 2021-2024 2.8%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway 2007 shareholder letter and financial statements

Per-share pre-tax earnings have increased by 15.2% compounded annually for the entire period 1965-2024.

The growth rate of non-insurance earnings increased for each period until 2007 but has since then decreased meaningfully, while the growth rate for per-share investments has decreased each whole period. Buffett has been eager to point out that this is what will happen when the base of assets and earnings gets too large. To wit (from his 2007 shareholder letter):

Though these tables may help you gain historical perspective and be useful in valuation, they are completely misleading in predicting future possibilities. Berkshire's past record can't be duplicated or even approached. Our base of assets and earnings is now far too large for us to make outsized gains in the future.

Buffett reminded investors of the same dynamics in his shareholder letter from 2023 (emphasis added by author):

There remain only a handful of companies in this country capable of truly moving the needle at Berkshire, and they have been endlessly picked over by us and by others. Some we can value; some we can’t. And, if we can, they have to be attractively priced. Outside the U.S., there are essentially no candidates that are meaningful options for capital deployment at Berkshire. All in all, we have no possibility of eye-popping performance.

Excluding the earnings from the insurance operations, earnings per share increased by only 2.2% in 2024. Only BHE (energy) experienced growth during the period, while the growth rates of the other operating companies remained flat.

It's really tough for Berkshire to achieve "eye-popping" performance with their existing businesses. Due to the mature nature of the railroad (BNSF) and energy markets, it's reasonable to assume slow growth in the future (excluding add-on acquisitions). In my article from last year, I concluded that Berkshire needs the excellent capital allocation skills of Buffett and his investment managers to outperform the S&P 500. Now that Buffett has announced he is stepping down as CEO by the end of the year, it falls to Greg Abel to gain new insights on where to allocate capital. Hopefully he's the man for the job. Otherwise, I find it very unlikely that Berkshire could beat the market in the long run.

The two sources of value can be further divided into four segments: Insurance, BNSF, BHE, as well as manufacturing, service, and retail.

Insurance

Insurance underwriting had an even better year than last year's outstanding performance, with after-tax profits of $9.0 billion (compared to $5.4 billion in 2023), corresponding to an average cost of float of less than zero. As long as the cost of float is lower than the alternative cost of funding, the insurance underwriting operations are performing well.

Over the last 20 years, Berkshire has had a lower cost of funding compared to, for example, the 10-year Treasury yield at year-end. Even more impressive is the fact that in 18 of these 20 years, Berkshire's cost of float has been below zero.

The funds from insurance have been cost-free more often than not, going back to when Berkshire first entered the insurance business in 1967 with the purchase of National Indemnity.

Year Underwriting earnings ($ millions) Average float ($ millions) Cost of float Year-end yield on 10-year treasuries
2016 1,370 89,500 less than zero 2.4%
2017 -2,219 102,500 2.2% 2.5%
2018 1,566 118,500 less than zero 2.7%
2019 325 126,000 less than zero 1.8%
2020 657 133,500 less than zero 0.9%
2021 870 142,500 less than zero 1.5%
2022 -30 155,500 0.1% 3.9%
2023 5,428 166,500 less than zero 3.9%
2024 9,021 170,000 less than zero 4.6%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

GEICO has been on a strong run during 2023-2024 and is still going strong in 2025. BH Primary had a good year with an underwriting profit of $855 million, although 2025 started with a loss. BHRG had an excellent year (again) with underwriting profits of $2,738 million, although 2025 started with a loss for BHRG as well.

I believe that the year 2024 was the top for Berkshire's insurance earnings, at least for a couple of years, as there is evidence that the insurance P&C market is turning soft in 2025. If Berkshire is to grow its earnings in 2025 and beyond, it must come from its non-insurance businesses.

Pre-tax earnings ($ million)

 GEICO BH Primary BHRG
2016 462 657 1,012
2017 -310 719 -3,648
2018 2,449 670 -1,109
2019 1,506 383 -1,472
2020 3,428 110 -2,700
2021 1,259 607 -755
2022 -1,880 393 1,465
2023 3,635 1,374 1,904
2024 7,813 855 2,738

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

The float has been used for investments in equity and fixed-income securities, which in turn produce investment income. Pre-tax investment income increased to $16.7 billion (~45% growth year-over-year).

The increase stems from interest income, which increased ~90% from the prior year due to still-high interest rates. Dividends declined by ~5% due to Berkshire being a net seller of listed equities.

Berkshire owned about $286 billion of T-bills at the end of 2024 and increased its holdings to $306 billion at the end of Q1 2025. The 1-year treasury rate has decreased compared to last year and is now about 4%. The running yield on the current T-bills is about $12.2 billion.

As I've mentioned numerous times, I hope that Greg Abel and his investment managers make an 'elephant' acquisition i.e. an operating company/listed stock that has the potential to earn 15% on invested capital annually, rather than the 4% that can be earned on T-bills currently.

Non-insurance businesses

This segment comprises all of Berkshire's operating companies that are not included in the insurance segment. The by far largest of these are Berkshire Hathaway Energy (BHE) and the railroad company Burlington Northern Santa Fe (BNSF).

In addition to these, there's a hodgepodge of other businesses, the largest of which are Marmon (part of the Industrial segment), Clayton Homes (Building products), McLane, Berkshire Hathaway Automotive (Retailing), NetJets, FlightSafety, and TTI (all three part of the Service segment). Pilot is a sizable new wholly owned company as well (part of the energy segment).

BNSF

Year Pre-tax earnings ($ millions)

Tangible net worth ($ millions)

 Pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth Pre-tax earnings on average carrying value
2015 6,775 35,451 - -
2016 5,693 37,793 15.5% 11.1%
2017 6,328 39,289 16.4% 11.9%
2018 6,863 39,754 17.4% 12.6%
2019 7,250 40,284 18.1% 13.2%
2020 6,792 40,378 16.8% 12.3%
2021 7,861 43,258 18.8% 13.9%
2022 7,708 43,678 17.7% 13.2%
2023 6,614 44,270 15.0% 11.2%
2024 6,648 44,153 15.0% 11.2%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

BNSF's asset base remained flat, and the earnings power on this asset base has been around 15-19%. Although earnings growth is slow and highly dependent on GDP, the earnings on average tangible net worth have been fair throughout the period outlined above.

In 2024, pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth remained at 15.0%. As Berkshire bought BNSF at a fairly large premium to net worth, represented by the goodwill item on Berkshire's balance sheet (of which $15.4 billion is allocated to BNSF), the earnings on average carrying value are reduced to 11-14%.

Although BNSF's cash flow from operations is positive, a large chunk of this is allocated to BNSF's maintenance and growth capital expenditures. I believe that the remainder is then sent up to headquarters for reallocation to other profitable opportunities.

BHE

Year After-tax earnings ($ millions) Tangible net worth ($ millions) After-tax earnings on average tangible net worth After-tax earnings on average carrying value
2015 - 31,219 - -
2016 2,507 32,828 7.8% 6.1%
2017 2,351 26,836 7.9% 6.0%
2018 2,924 29,600 10.4% 7.7%
2019 3,144 34,269 9.8% 7.5%
2020 3,544 45,239 8.9% 7.0%
2021 4,446 48,974 9.4% 7.5%
2022 4,775 51,998 9.5% 7.7%
2023 2,962 55,176 5.5% 4.5%
2024 4,163 59,800 7.2% 6.0%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

BHE's yearly capital expenditures are over twice the amount of BNSF's (in the year 2024, BHE spent $9.0 billion on maintenance and expansion capex while BNSF spent $3.7 billion). BHE has a significantly larger asset base than BNSF, although its earnings are smaller.

Due to the nature of the sector in which BHE operates (providing essential services to consumers and customers), the returns on most of its businesses are regulated, i.e., there are no outsized returns to be had here. The flip side of the coin is that BHE can deploy vast amounts of capital at stable and fair returns.

This has been true in the past, but as Warren described in last year's letter and discussed during this year's annual meeting, the regulatory landscape for utilities has changed in certain states, effectively preventing utilities from earning their expected return on equity. In addition, Warren has expressed concern about whether it's worth committing additional capital to Western states that are vulnerable to climate change phenomena, such as wildfires.

The earnings on average tangible net worth increased to 7.2% in 2024. As in BNSF's case, Berkshire bought BHE and its subsidiaries at premiums to net worth, which is why earnings on average carrying value is only 6.0%. The increase in 2024 was due to the wildfire provisions of $1.6 billion in 2023, i.e., estimated damages that potentially have to be paid in the future.

In BHE's case, I don't believe any of the excess cash can be sent to Berkshire for reallocation as the business is so capital-intensive. It actually seems to consume everything it generates and more, as after-tax earnings in 2024 were less than half of the capital expenditures ($4 billion in earnings versus capital expenditures of over $9 billion). The amount of cash needed is staggering, and as the regulatory environment remains uncertain, BHE could face significant headwinds in the years to come, including increased wildfire-related claims and lower returns on equity.

Manufacturing, Service and Retail

Year Pre-tax earnings ($ millions) Tangible net worth ($ millions) Pre-tax earnings on average tangible net worth Pre-tax earnings on average carrying value
2016 10,224 54,216 - -
2017 10,927 58,879 19.3% 11.3%
2018 12,308 65,535 19.8% 11.9%
2019 12,365 67,321 18.6% 11.5%
2020 10,889 60,605 17.0% 10.8%
2021 14,552 66,796 22.8% 15.2%
2022 16,219 65,359 24.5% 16.3%
2023 17,589 85,460 23.3% 15.5%
2024 16,843 92,145 19.0% 13.0%

Source: Berkshire Hathaway financial statements

Combined, this segment is the largest of the non-insurance businesses. However, there are over 60 companies included in this segment, so individually, they're significantly smaller than BNSF and BHE.

These businesses enjoy enviable economics, earning an average of 19.0% on their tangible net worth in 2024. As is the case with BNSF and BHE, Berkshire has acquired these businesses at a substantial premium to net worth, and the pre-tax earnings on average carrying value was 13.0% in 2024. The decrease from the previous years has to due with Pilot's inclusion in the figures. Due to the capital intensive nature of its business model it drags down the returns for the whole group.

Intrinsic business value

Charlie and Warren used to measure how their per-share intrinsic value developed over time in comparison to the S&P 500. Intrinsic value is, however, an elusive concept. A good proxy for it is per-share book value and how it has developed.

Book value obviously understates intrinsic value. The amount by which intrinsic value exceeds book value (in relative terms) doesn't change substantially from year to year, however, which is why per-share book value is a good yardstick for intrinsic value.

Per-share book value increased in 2024 by about 16.6% while BRK's share price increased by 27.1%. If we assume that BRK's share price approximates intrinsic value, it implies that the ratio of intrinsic value to book value increased during 2024. It's, however, a leap to assume that BRK's share price performance during a single year would approximate the change in intrinsic value.

In the long term, however, the share price to per-share book value ratio should resemble the per-share intrinsic value to per-share book value ratio as the share price should converge to per-share intrinsic value (with large swings in the interim). In 2024, the P/B increased from 1.37x to 1.51x. We need, however, a longer-term metric to conclude what the normal premium should be.

Below I have included a graph that shows how P/B has developed over the last 10 years in addition to the 10-year median P/B. The long-term median has remained fairly stable at around 1.40x. The current valuation has risen quite much, ~13% above the long-term trend, indicating overvaluation, although it has decreased from the 10-year peak.

Data by YCharts

I've also updated my valuation of BRK, which I described in more detail here.

My current estimate of Berkshire's intrinsic value is about $1,072 billion. From my year-ago valuation of $912 billion, the value has increased by $151 billion. The most significant changes on the operating side are insurance underwriting (which I don't think is sustainable) and BHE. On the non-operating side, cash and T-bills increased by $180 billion, while listed investments decreased by $79 billion due to Berkshire's net selling of equities. The proceeds were used to buy T-bills. The difference between the increase in cash and the decrease in equities of $101 billion is primarily due to the fact that equities generated positive returns during the year, in addition to the cash generated from other businesses and T-bills.

At BRK's current market capitalization of $1,036 billion, my estimate is 4% higher than the market cap and therefore doesn't fill the requirement of a 20% margin of safety. This is why I rate Berkshire Hathaway a hold at these prices. In addition, assuming insurance underwriting earnings decrease during 2025, it will have a negative impact on the valuation going forward.

Finally, I like to look at how the gains of the equity securities have developed during the period. As we can see, 2024 was another stellar year for Buffett with $53 billion in equity gains. There will be large swings in this segment in the future, and one should consider the cumulative change over time to get a proper picture of Berkshire's investment success. During Buffett's 60-year period, it's just an extraordinary achievement. Zooming in on the cumulative gain since the start of 2018, it looks very decent as well: over $218 billion of cumulative equity gains!

data.title: AGNC: Better Not To Fight Against The Market (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
AGNC: Q2's Steep Volatility Was A Timely Reminder

mREIT investors endured a challenging early Q2, as Trump's tariff turmoil wreaked havoc in the market and led to a de-risking posture, which also widened spreads unexpectedly. As a result, it also led a hammering in the stock of AGNC Investment Corp. (NASDAQ:AGNC), notwithstanding its leadership among its peers, with a market cap of almost $10B.

Accordingly, in price performance terms, AGNC fell more than 25% from its 2025 highs before bottoming out in April. Additionally, while the broad market has taken out new highs, investor sentiments have remained languid in AGNC, as the stock continues to trade below its long-term valuation average while struggling for traction to regain its upward cadence toward its 2025 peak.

Hence, I think it's opportune for me to provide a timely update for AGNC investors on whether they should consider seizing the current initiative to partake in what seems like a highly attractive dividend yield of 15%. Following my AGNC writeup in October 2024, the mREIT has performed reasonably okay, slightly trailing the S&P 500's (SPX) (SPY) with a total return of 7.5%. Hence, the magnitude of its dividend yield has arguably fortified investors from an even more pervasive hammering, as the company recorded a mixed earnings release, imputed to Q2's unanticipated market volatility.

AGNC: Structural Underperformance Likely To Stymie Near-Term Recovery Efforts

While the downtrend bias in AGNC's tangible book value per share (Q2: $7.81) over the past year has arguably hurt investor sentiments, the company's core earnings per share (Q2: $0.38) should still sustain its current capital allocation. The company has also primed its portfolio, anticipating interest rate reductions moving forward. However, due to the decline in its tangible book value per share, it also spurred a reversal in its economic return to -1% in the previous quarter. While it's obviously not a welcomed development, I believe it's too early for us to ascertain whether it represents something more structural or a temporal impact on its portfolio, given the market volatility that afflicted its tangible book value in early Q2.

Furthermore, AGNC management highlighted that it had already regained "about 1%" of its tangible book value per share in July (post-monthly dividend accrual). Hence, unless you expect that the market sentiments could worsen, leading to another fallout over the next few months, I believe it's reasonable for us to contemplate that another fallout might not be the base case.

In addition, while the Q2 market stress impinged on its economic return, the mREIT hasn't faced significant challenges in meeting its dividend payout of $0.36 per share from its Q2 core earnings. Moreover, the company retains the flexibility of deploying the ATM equity proceeds raised at a "significant premium" in Q2 to bolster its forward earnings potential. Also, management assured us that the current leverage (about 7.6x tangible equity) still supports the generation of a "mid-to-high $0.30s to low-to-mid-$0.40s range" moving forward to secure its dividend payouts to income investors.

AGNC's Declining Book Value Per Share Says It All

Nevertheless, the portfolio is still expected to undergo a decline in tangible book value per share through 2026, although it's expected to finish higher than what it recorded in Q2.

Hence, it seems like analysts have maintained their downcast posture on what appears to be a structural downtrend on the company's ability to add long-term shareholder value, consistent with the downward sloping price action seen in its share price in the following chart.

Accordingly, the ongoing equity raise (although priced at a premium to TBVPS) hasn't helped the company overturn the glaring downward spiral examined, as seen in its tangible book value per share trajectory. Hence, I don't think a massive valuation upgrade/re-rating opportunity could be forthcoming, as the market increasingly demands a higher margin of safety for its underperformance.

Buy Or Sell AGNC Stock?

With AGNC's forward yield now at the 15% mark, it's already substantively over its 10Y average of 12.2%. Yet, some could argue that the borrow short (repo) and lend long business model (long-duration Agency MBS assets—with implicit government guarantee) hasn't quite worked as well as anticipated. And the downward trending portfolio value could continue to "eat into" the high dividend yields, widening the discount rate (or dividend yields) that investors require to hold AGNC over time.

Nevertheless, some positive aspects should emerge in the next 12 months. Catalysts such as expectations of further interest rate cuts (lowering its short-term repo borrowing rates) and the continuation of the yield curve inversion should also benefit its long-term asset pricing.

In addition, with the market environment having returned to a less volatile state as Trump goes into deal-making mode with America's principal trading partners, I think the market has come to terms with what is construed as more normalized market conditions. While the ongoing trade talks could still take a turn for the worse, I believe the thesis of "escalate to de-escalate" is increasingly noted in Trump's playbook, plausibly helping to calm the nerves of AGNC investors, previously perturbed with the spreads widening experienced in early April.

Hence, I believe the mREIT should benefit from these near-term catalysts, although I'm much less certain about whether its ability to drive a long-term recovery is still sound. With a persistent downward trend in its tangible book value per share pressuring its ability to outperform through future share issuances, the market is expected to maintain a relatively high dividend yield to offset such potential losses and dilution.

And while the company believes the 7.6x leverage ratio still offers sufficient room to maneuver, the uncertainties in the business environment and tariff-related impacts on the overall economy could persist. Hence, I don't think it's prudent for the company to engage in even higher leverage to juice its portfolio value, with short-term rates potentially also being hampered by the potential tariff pass through.

Therefore, it seems like even mREIT leaders like AGNC might have to contend with a fair amount of ambivalence, suggesting the structural headwinds resisting a more robust recovery could persist for some time to come.

Rating: Downgrade to Hold.
